items.title: 5-fold CV to compare ridge penalties
items.scenario: A team predicts house prices with ridge regression. They run 5-fold CV on the training set for α ∈ {0.01, 0.1, 1, 10} and pick α with lowest mean RMSE. Mean RMSE at α=1 is 0.42 with std 0.03 across folds.
items.explanation: CV averages performance across five different train/val partitions, reducing sensitivity to one random 80/20 split. Final α is chosen on CV; a separate test set reports sign-off RMSE once.

items.title: Stratified CV for rare fraud class
items.scenario: Fraud detection has 0.8% positive labels. Plain k-fold once put zero fraud cases in a validation fold. Stratified 5-fold keeps ~0.8% positives in every fold; mean PR-AUC becomes reportable.
items.explanation: Stratification ensures each fold reflects the global class mix so metrics like recall and PR-AUC are not undefined or wildly noisy.

items.title: Random forest depth tuning with repeated CV
items.scenario: An analyst compares `max_depth` 5 vs 15 for a random forest on 2,000 labeled rows. Single 5-fold CV favored depth 15 by 0.01 AUC; repeated 5-fold with 3 repeats shows depth 5 wins on mean AUC with lower fold std.
items.explanation: Repeated k-fold reduces optimism from one fold boundary; reporting mean and std exposes unstable winners.

items.title: Nested CV for unbiased algorithm comparison
items.scenario: Researchers compare logistic regression vs gradient boosting on 800 patients. Outer 5-fold estimates generalization; inner 3-fold within each outer train fold picks learning rate. They report outer-fold mean AUC only—never the inner best score.
items.explanation: Inner CV tunes; outer CV evaluates. Using inner best scores as the reported metric would leak tuning into the estimate.
